title: 2025/08/20 - MA (Part 2)
description: Wednesday 20 August 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee (Part 2) Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlements Hearing of evidence Submitter list: Land Information New Zealand - Toitū Te Whenua Gaye Searancke, Te Tumu Whakarae/Chief Executive Jan Pierce, Kaihautū, Customer Delivery Dione Payne, Head of Māori-Crown Relations Sonya Wikitera, Head of Crown Property
descriptionSummary: (Part 2). Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlements. Land Information New Zealand - Toitū Te Whenua

chunks.text: So, uh, welcome to all this afternoon. Thank you very much for coming. And, uh, if I may just start, uh, by, um, sort of perhaps passing a few comments from the committee. Obviously this area here of ensuring that public entities namely the Crown, but also others including, uh, local government mm-hmm.

Um, committing or completing their, their commitments under treaty settlements is a very important matter to us. And, uh, this, uh, particular briefing is, I think we've already premised you, is is primarily based on the report produced by the Auditor General's office. And it's got a number of recommendations there itself.

Um, also thank you very much for the document that you provided, and we've also got it online there. Uh, and I'll imagine you'll be talking to that to, to somewhat, I also acknowledge that your entity itself with Lins is, uh, a very busy one in, in with regards to this particular space outside of, uh, well, from my read, the, uh, outside of the Department of Conservation, you have a huge number of commitments there.

And I must acknowledge you've done incredibly well with the number you've already, uh, completed. But nevertheless, it's really important to us to see how we're going or where the challenges are, uh, potentially, uh, what are we doing to make sure that we keep up with the commitments that the settlements themselves, uh, the right of the wrongs of the past.

Mm-hmm. And we don't want to sit in a position where we've still got wrongs going forward, so to speak. So with that very, uh, beginning, um, I'll leave it to you to do a bit of an introduction and, and talk to you and we'll follow that with some, uh, uh, questions, uh, for you. And we've got about 45 minutes that we've allocated for this, this time with today.
So away you go. Nevermind. Thank you very much, Mr. Chair.

I'm the Chief Executive of Land Information New Zealand. Actually had my sixth anniversary of in the role yesterday. Oh, congratulations. Also losing my voice. Couldn't speak yesterday. It's come back just briefly for this meeting. Um, I thought given that, um, because it's a bit unreliable, I would just make some very brief comments and then hand over to the experts, um, and the team who will introduce themselves and, and speak to some different areas, uh, as introductory remarks.

Next go. Very pleased to take your questions. Um, well, thank you very much for the opportunity to speak to the committee today, uh, about our work in treaty settlement commitment delivery, um, and in particular about the improvements that we are making and will continue to make, um, following what we thought was a very helpful, um, ultimately and quite, um, in incisive report on the office, the Auditor General.

Um, what we'll cover today is, uh, our role in Lins. We just give you a, an overview of that, um, the recommendations in, in the report and how, um, we are responding to those, what we are doing and what we were already doing. Uh, I wanna talk a little bit about our rfr quite of refusal and memorials, which are very important part of, of, uh, settlement commitments.

The reporting and tracking, um, the nitty gritty of making sure that, uh, that cement obligations are delivered on, uh, a bit of delivery detail. And then I thought we'll close with just letting you know how the accountability for delivery has translated into my performance agreement as a ce.
Very good. Thank you. I'll hand over to Jan. Um,

I'm the, uh, customer delivery. So both the Crown property, which you'll hear about and our Maori crown teams sit under my responsibility, um, and I report to gay. So, um, at Lins we have, as you, as you've identified, an important role to play in supporting the Crown to honor its commitments to Iwi and Maori.

Um, we take this responsibility, um, really seriously and we are honored that we can be part of righting the wrongs of the past, as you have identified. Um, we have a particular focus on treaty of Y settlement commitments involving land. This includes management of properties held in the Land Bank for future treat treaty settlements and administration of deeds of settlement, and registering rights of first refusal memorials on land titles as part of its review report.

In April this year, the audit, the Office of the Auditor General, provided recommendations to public service agencies in ways that government can improve treaty settlement obligations and how they delivered the review contained one recommendation specific to us. This was Land Information New Zealand works to ensure that there is a system in place so that rights of first refusal, refusal memorials are correctly placed on land title.

The review also made a number of general recommendations for public service agencies in the govern governing entities, which are relevant to lends. These include increasing both leadership capability and delivery expectations on chief executives who have settlement commitments and responsibilities.

And again, we'll talk about that more at the end as a public sector organization or settlement commitments. We review how we plan to meet and monitor our settlement commitments, improving reporting through our annual annual report on our role and responsibilities in relation to settlements, including providing accessible information on the status of our commitments and any issues outstanding.

In March, 2025, we commenced a review of how we work across lends to deliver treaty settlement obligations. This review identified both good practices and areas for improvement. We have now set clear expectations of roles and responsibilities across the organization in relation to settlement commitments.

We are implementing standardized processes and tools with a focus on improving the registration of rights of first refusal memorials, and improving our delivery and detailing reporting of settlement commitments. To help us achieve this, we are also strengthening our people's capability.

Our plan focuses on building our people's understanding of New Zealand's history of TEI or Ang Teo, Maori and Tikanga. In Kawa, we have identified the level of capability required for each role based on its level of engagement with Maori and our ensuring more tailored training and capability development to actively build stronger relationships with Maori.

We have a dedicated Maori crown relationships function that Deon will speak about soon, that engages with iwi and works closely with our service delivery program. So I'm gonna pass on to Sonya now to talk more about our rights and first refusal. Sonya Wiki. Um, I am the head of Crown Property and ua, uh, the delivery arm, uh, of the treaty settlements.

Uh, and Dion will introduce herself as in the Maori crown relations, um, component. So, um, the recommendation from, um, the report talked about the first writer of refusal and we've provided information, um, in the pack, uh, on page two and on page three. And I just wanted to highlight a couple of things, uh, which is actually in the pack, uh, that the, uh, agreed treaty settlements, uh, grant iwi the rights of first refusal.

Uh, and they're specifically on properties or on areas that the Crown owned at the time, of course, to ensure that we, uh, safeguard iwi interests later if the Crown chooses to get rid of that property. Uh, we, uh, each settlement, uh, is quite unique. Um, and I'm not gonna get into too much detail unless that's part of your question.

Uh, and they talk about our list, um, RFR. So we have list properties or area, and so they, they can actually be quite complex. Mm-hmm. Uh, but we need to work through those. Uh, and we have, uh, to make sure all of those unique, uh, things have been taken into account. And if there is a title to be able to register the first right of refusal on that title.

Uh, we have been, uh, doing some improvement work, um, on the guidelines. So we provide guidelines, uh, for other agencies. Um, and what we are looking to do is make sure that those guidelines for each specific treaty settlement are available for other agencies to use as soon as that treaty settlement is finalized, so that all of the agencies understand, um, how or how they should behaving or what they should be doing.
Uh, if however there is a title, uh, then the lens people will put the memorial in the title system. And I think I might stop there. Okay. Um, and hand over to,

Um, I'm the head of Maori crown relations and my role is the oversights of treaty settlements, um, by bringing all of our together and coordinating effectively amongst all of our teams that do it, great amount of work. Um, if you'll see on slide four, we might just pause at slide three 'cause that's what, um, Sonia was talking about in the total number of memorials here.

But on slide four, um, our commitments are visible externally through the TE portal. And as you would've seen in the slides that we have just under 3000 of them, those commitments vary in size. They can be very simple, such as appointing a valuer for one of the properties, or can be very complex, which includes large scale multi-year projects, demolishing or remediating buildings earthworks.

Um, so we have quite a variety. And with those commitments then they can get stuck in some places as we work through the complexity of that. But in 2023, if I may, um, just note, we did a big review of TETA internally and we were able to make sure that all of the commitments were in the right place.

And at the beginning of this year, as part of a bigger piece of work, we went through and closed out a number of those, which is why we've had quite a significant movement from the beginning of the year with our commitments to now. Um, this is actually, even though it's only a couple of weeks old, the commitments have moved by another a hundred. And that kind of speaks to how often we're in the portal, making sure that we are updating daily.

Um, we coordinate with Sonya's team so we get all of their information. And what this does is it gives iwi and other people assurance or reassurance that we are completing the MAHI as we are going. And we've actually increased our standard of visibility by ensuring that there are references within the notations.

So if we've transferred something, there is a total reference in there, so we don't have to do that. But as part of our improvement process, we thought that that would just be easier for Iwi or PSGs to be able to identify those. So as a part that was mentioned in the OAG, I mean at that time and place, that was certainly something we needed to improve on.

And I believe that we've done that really well. One of the places that we, that we've done particularly well would be the delivery issues. So we have leaders in both our teams that deal with all of the escalations. So we had a much, much larger number last year of delivery issues and we've been able to get, um, work through them all the ones that are there now though, uh, with regard to our CFLs, which I'll talk to shortly.

So just to note that, um, our commitment numbers are also included in our annual report with an explanation about each of those sections. So they're yet to be triggered though, if I could just point to that, um, that number is there because there are other agencies or ESGs that need to do a mahi first, and then our mahi happens after they've done their bit.

So that will always be around a hundred to 200 because it just requires somebody else to do something. So those are generally out of our, so if I could go to slide five, um, with regard to the CFLs that I mentioned before. So these are the CFLs that are overdue.

So the breach CFLs, if I, if I may say. And, um, we've done quite well in moving stairs along. We moved one along in July, so was returned back to, um, I'm really pleased to be able to say, and we did everything we could to have it transferred before this, this morning.

But we also, I have no idea how much pressure we brought. It just would've been nice, but thank you very much. Committee. In any case, um, we are really pleased to be able to advise that RAI and Queen Charlotte, which is at the top of the south island, all of that documentation has been signed and it is currently assigned for the last part, which will transfer the forest back to, um, the even that those relate to.

So that number I physically saw the, um, we did the inches thick signed documentation that come back because yesterday in our Hamilton office Mm. And, and myself, Jan and, um, gay has put a lot of pressure, a lot of pressure. Um, but it's such a complex, um, there's such complex dealings that if you think of a normal dealing, which is quite a thin documentation, this, this has a thick folder aligned to it.

So, um, our people are trying earnestly, uh, our land registry people are trying and earnestly to get that done for us. That does mean that there are still some breach CFLs there, sorry, over G CFLs. Um, as you can see, some are in active litigation. We won't be able to move those until, um, that litigation concludes. There is one there, Ru Ator that has gone back to the MA land court for, um, for an order.

But the issue that we were dealing with has now been resolved. So we need that to come back out of the Maori land court and then we can continue to transfer that for us across. And then there are three others that we are working with Iwi at the moment to resolve. So those ones, though, those last three are issues that require another agency to do something.

So we still need to wait for other parties to be able to do their part. Um, we don't like that they're overdue, but we are trying as hard as we can to make that work. And then obviously we have internal reporting that we do both up to TU and the CEO. So they're abreast of all of the mahi that's going on in Maori crime relations.

And part of that is also reporting on the effectiveness of our squads. So we have squads that wrap around a, uh, treaty settlements and then that squad monitors all of our, um, dealings internally and externally, our comm with Iwi. And so we report all of that through to Jan and Gabe so that they are, um, aware of that.

And then from there we also report that in Teta and then on which to the annual report. So we've done quite a bit of mahi in that space to make sure that we give as much visibility given the importance of this mahi to the organization. I might just talk to, if we go to the next slide. So we did provide, um, committee just some information around those Crown Forest licenses that weren't overdue.

Uh, and what we have learn, and of course through part of the review is that we needed to start earlier, uh, on organizing those Crown Forest license, uh, titles and documentation to be worked through with Iwi. Uh, and this just demonstrates that, you know, we've learnt and we've improved. Mm-hmm. And so actually we have transferred a number of CFLs to Iwi before, uh, the five year, um, time limit.

Mm-hmm. One the month after. That's good. So doing quite a lot of work upfront to make sure that, um, you know, there's ongoing engagement with Iwi, uh, and that we can get that, um, get that title transferred. Okay. Thank you guys. Great. Um, the last, uh, part that I thought I'd just, um, suppose to, to demonstrate to the committee that, um, the report, um, has been taken seriously and that we have, um, I suppose up to the accountability for delivery is that my performance expectations

as a CE now have, um, specific references to delivery and treaty settlements, which is not the case for all the work that the agency does. So not all of our work is mentioned in there. So this has been identified and prioritized. Um, they include ensuring that all updates and, and inputs into are consistent, timely, and accurate.

So the reporting, uh, ensuring that Crown Forest license is not affected by disputes or litigation or related party delays are transferred to Iwi. So that's staying on top of the current and future CFLs and working, um, really hard to resolve the ways that are stuck, continuing to support and implement treaty settlement processes in a timely manner to ensure organization and system arrangements, support deliveries, ensuring the organization stays resourced and ready to implement future settlements.

And prioritize meeting treaty settlement commitments, including reviewing how Lynn's plans to meet and monitor commitments and improving the information in our annual report about progress. So those are the four specific conventions in my performance expectations. Were those performance expectations, uh, was that you proactively, um, seeking that? Or was that delivered By I do that with the commission, but we suggested most of the detail of the wording and they, they, but they asked that it reflect the e expectations and findings of the OAG.

So it was picked up by the commission and also picked up by us and together we after those. Do you have specific budget lines, uh, that are associated with this particular mahiki being on top of this particular path? No, I mean, it's included in the, in the funding and appropriation and the Crown property, um, and part of our work.

Um, and part of the Maori crown thing was also funded, I think the weekly, but also we've allocated what you might call collective overhead costs Right. To the work to support this work, um, that the treaty settlement commitments. And has budget been a limiting factor in you being able to deliver the good stuff you're delivering today in the past? Like what has led to this change outside of this Prioritization?

Yeah. Yep. I think in focus and probably, um, learning where to have the expertise, um, uh, my team's responsible for the delivery of the settlements, but having, uh, Dion's team who's across making sure that all the settlements from end, you know, start to end, uh, in all of the various, uh, commitments through, uh, actually being, um, you know, being realized.

So it's quite good. We've got our, our own watchdog, uh, internally. Mm. I think also we've really worked hard to align amongst ourselves how we could work better. Mm-hmm. Um, we were in very different systems, so we've using common tools. We have the ability for our leaders to work very closely.

Sonya and I work closely and travel together at, at times just to cool some, some things down. But I think it's just been a real willingness to know that. And I, if I can say the CFLs gave us a bit of a adult that we had these breaches. Mm-hmm. And I think we all wanted to do better than that. So what we've done in the last 12 to 18 months has been quite a big shift.

I think the OAG came in just as we were at the beginning of that change. Mm-hmm. So we've been able to realize those, um, improvements. And this has just helped us focus on a couple of things. But we were already well on our way, I think by the time we saw the report, we were quite shocked at how we'd already started. I just quickly looked at the date you started your review of March.

Mm-hmm. This report didn't come out, so you'd obviously done something a little bit. Right. Okay. Open up to questions from, and you went of clarity for me. Great. Yeah, just, um, that's, uh, thank you so much. Um, that's, um, hugely encouraging, um, to hear the detail of the work you've been doing. And thank you very much for this document in advance. Um, you've already touched on, um, on, on this, but just running, given the fact that you've literally only got five, um, with that experiencing delays, are you able to run through quickly what those five,

I know you touched briefly on you, you said three of them are to do with other departments. So they're the ones that are in litigation are part of the spinal award litigation. So that's, and that's part of the high court and or Okay. So we can't do anything with that. And that's all five, that's the Waka two? No, that's for, there's three Three outta The five. Yeah. Okay. And then the other three one's gone back into the Maori land court.

So we can't proceed until the judge provides his last commentary on the easements that were put in place. Yep. 'cause they were easements for land alongside the crown forest. Sure. And then the other two, there is a, um, oh, I don't wanna throw them under the bus. Um, but there's been some encroachments, um, oh, okay. On the sides of the, um, crown forest.

So we need to legally sort that out. Right. And, and fairness to the iwi, they would prefer that we sorted that out. Yeah. Okay. And so that's, that's that part. And then the last one can't move until that one moves. Gotcha. Right. So what I can reassure you though is that we are in touch with our iwi all the time and trying to find ways to resolve that.

Right. But because it does involve other agencies, it does mean that we need to coordinate with other agencies to progress something that is well outside our control, but we do it anyway. So it just makes sense. How do you work proactively with those other agencies? 'cause I would assume that Iwi or the post settlement groups look at Lins, it's your particular accountability and, and doesn't matter whether they go into another spot, how do you proactively try and best position those other agencies that you rely on with the delivery to be able

to do it in a timely, efficient manner? So I think if I was talking about the forest, we've just done a little bit of jiggle around who is looking after that space. Um, I'm quite bolshy, so I don't mind, I don't mind ringing having a Hui with Iwi. That wasn't a now No, no. But I don't mind ringing iwi and with our, with the relationship partners that sit in my team is going, let's just go and visit and find out what all the issues are.

I don't mind getting a growling. I mean, I think it's, it's better to know than to assume. And then in doing that, then we start to work out a work program with Iwi around how we can resolve it with the others. We've met recently with Auckland Transport, where, where there's, where there's something going on. Um, and then we've resolved to work with Auckland Transport someone in, uh, Sonya's team that will deal, deal with some of the ni n****y little parts.

And then I'm involved and my relationship partner's involved as the oversight to make sure we know it's moving. Now, it's not to say that we could have done this sooner. I think if we attack all of them at once, we, it becomes a capacity issue. But we have a very strong plan on what we can move, what we can move now, and that will what will take more time.

So the Waka two ones obviously are just gonna take time. So you will continue to see these as breach CFLs until all of that's done. But at least we can talk to that, we can explain why that is there. Mm-hmm. And I know very intimately why the other three are there as well. Just before I ask for another question, I just pass a comment. I think it's important that it's seen that you are proactive in a degree.

People can understand and, you know, communications is really important. They can understand that sometimes things don't go smoothly as you, you anticipate, but if you're seen to be procrastinating Yes. Uh, then that will actually derive a, a different outcome with regards to relationships and all that sort Of, or if we, or if we go quiet. Yes. Even if we are, even if we are beavering away and inside our own organization, if we don't communicate it, um, people can lightly assume that not much is happening.

I agree. Even if that's not case Question. The other part of that, just before you move on, sorry, is also, um, within the teams, them working closely together and actually helping Iwi to understand their part of it as well. Right. Rather than sitting back and saying that's yours. We've, we really actively on, on assisting Iwi to, to actually do the things they, they need to do as well.

Adrian. Um, some claimant groups are quite surprised that it can take up to five years to transfer. Um, see if they should be shorter. Well, I see you managed to, um, cut it, actually cut it down. Yeah. Which is great. But, um, like a lot of things, timeframes end up being targets rather than actual, And they are, um, The timeframe is set within the settlement legislation, so it does allow for that period.

And I think what we have learnt is if we don't start Quick enough, we haven't been, we don't good at, um, reaching that target. Um, should it be less, I think it depends. It depends on the complexity. I think with, um, if we think about the forest, it was one iwi and it was those forests were going to one iwi.

Yeah. When there are multiple iwi, that's not an excuse, but rather that it gets a little bit more complex. Yeah. But despite that, if on average doing it in 18 months, wouldn't it be better to just make it 18 months with the knowledge that some will go overdue anyway?

I think that the numbers would demonstrate that when we start earlier, we can do it quicker. And I think that is the learning from the past that it was a target rather than a, than a, you know, the, the real time it took. Um, that said, there is a, a ex, there's been an extremely high amount of priority Yes.

Given to achieve these numbers. So that would have to be maintained. And to your question about the funding, we have made decisions to prioritize this work, which means that some work that we want us to do with them that is not about treaty settlements, we haven't prioritized yet. Well, that's good for us to hear because, um, I think that's something that committee, we need to note that it's dependent on, um, resourcing to actually do, you know, carry out these functions which are legally binding. Yes. Yeah, Exactly.

So an example of that might be we get a number of requests for geospatial data or based data that Lens provides. Now Iwi would love us to help them do that, but to do that fully, we would have to stop that. Mm-hmm. So there, we don't like to do that. So where we can fit it in, we do, but it does mean if this is the priority that is in all of our mm-hmm.

You know, accountabilities and legally we're required to do, then we start with that first and then we move across to what we can do after that. Can I just ask a supplementary to this before we come to your room? Mm-hmm. Your, um, changing of priorities, I'll call it over time, but I think there's been a shift here with the whole organization.

I wanna understand if I can, what made you lead to that change? Because we have a challenge ourselves in talking to other ces of other organizations about what, what's gonna motivate them to change their priorities? What lead you to change yours? Well, for, for Lens, if we are really, uh, direct about it, our dealings with land is our core business.

And this is really close to our core business. It's not a di it's actually we diverted from, in a way, our core business in relation to land by not prioritizing this work or maybe for other agencies, it's additional rather than what they might have described as core.

So, so for Lynn's, for example, land bank properties, we've, we've held onto land bank properties prior to settlements, and that is part of our core business to hold that land. Then, then the settlements are achieved, the land is then transferred. So we actually, in a way, we just, we're continuing what is part of our core work.

There's no point us holding onto land that we shouldn't be holding onto. Right. And so then we transfer it. The CFLs are a little different, but, so I think there's been two things. The shock of the breach, the shock of those CFL breaches mm-hmm. Was significant. Yeah. Um, the assertiveness with which we were, which we were held to account by iwi for those breaches, you were receiving complaints?

Oh yeah. We, we did, when, when we sent the, the, the key one, which was Riverhead Mm. And we talked to Iwi about, we can't get this done in time, would you agree to an extension? We heard from them in no uncertain terms, the impact that that would have on us. Okay. And so that was really clear that their expectation was that the contract would be honored and that we weren't going to honor it.

And it was, it was not acceptable to them. So really that was a, that was probably one of the key change. And what does Yeah. Okay. Remark. Um, funnily enough, it kind of, um, intertwines No, no, no, it's okay. Um, uh, gay. Yes. Uh, I'm Chief Executive Kda. You mentioned that, um, in your opening cordo you said that, um, performance delivery, your performance delivery, um, it, it needs to include treaty, um, settlement obligations.

And I just want to ask, at what point within the last six years did that come into play? It's formally in my performance expectations this year for the first time it's been called out as a specific thing. Previously it would've been incorporated into deliver all the operational obligations of your department.

So it wouldn't have been identified as a specific area of work or a project in the same way. Um, but I think it that, you know, this year there's probably four key things that I need to deliver that are priorities. And this is one of the ones that's been called out as well as ensuring that my agency has the capability to work effectively with, with iwi across the board, not just in terms of settlements.

So that's probably, it is probably got more pointy in the last year, but I wouldn't say it's been absent in previous years. It just hasn't been as, um, specifically identified. Mm-hmm. Thank you, Scott. Um, thank you for talking about the data side and that it's not a particular priority at the moment, but I imagine that you're getting queries from, um, iwi Mariah about, particularly with sea level rise and, and climate impacts at risk, Mariah at risk.

Mm-hmm. Um, what, how, because this seems like quite a priority as well. How are we gonna manage these competing priorities? And, and that's one question. And is the relationship with the earth sciences previously GNSS going to enable that provision of, of, um, data points essentially to help adaptation and manage the risks for really important, really important sites?

How, I mean, it's a big question, but mm-hmm. Um, that's a good Question. We can break it up though. Let's talk about what is publicly available that is usable, because you talk about that Jan. And then we might talk about some specific engagement that we've had with Iwi around geospatial information. Mm-hmm. So, um, we were successful two years ago in the budget to, um, commence a project, um, to do coastal mapping.

So this is bringing together the, our Sea data along with, um, land data. There's a piece in the middle that doesn't get matched through those two, um, processes. And, um, so at the moment, and this will then enable organizations like Earth Science to do the modeling, what's the impact of flood?

Um, what's the impact of, um, tsunami, how far up the, you know, depending on the sea floor and what it looks like, if there's a shelf or, or the, um, coast, then the tsunami won't hit it, you know, come over so much, et cetera. So that project, um, is in its first year, we've just done the data gathering and that'll be available to everybody to enable, um, as you say, for councils for earth sciences to do that modeling and understand the impact of both, um, sea level rise, but also the land moves too.

And groundwater. Yeah. Yeah. So, um, because it's can be a double whammy. If the sea's coming up and the land's going down, you're actually gonna inundate a lot faster. So, um, so that project's happening at the moment. We did, um, flying season, so using, using, um, lidar at the metric lidar and land lidar.

And, um, that project did had a flying season last summer. 'cause obviously you can only fly in the summer. And, um, and we're just in the throes of processing that data now, and we're doing, uh, about 40% of the coastline, um, using, um, parts of the coast that either have assets or, um, built up areas.

Um, and that's the first stage of the project. So that'll help provide the data and then be able to do the analysis to understand the Impacts. And this is on top of the data that's already been available through the LIDAR program, which has been running for, what, six years or so? Yeah. Mm-hmm. Um, which covers a lot of inland waterways.

Yeah. So that's, that, that information is currently publicly available, the point venture with council. So Just do a quick follow. So thank you. That's great to hear.
